result:
There’s a cracked coffee mug on my desk that I can’t throw away. The handle broke clean off last winter when I slammed it down too hard after reading an email I didn’t like. The ceramic edge is sharp enough to draw blood, but I still drink from it every morning, curling my fingers around the body like it’s a bird with a broken wing that might remember how to fly. Maybe I keep it because I’m cheap, or maybe because I think if I survive one more year with this wounded thing at my center, I’ll prove I’m not entirely breakable myself.

Outside, the neighbor’s kid is learning trumpet. Same five notes, over and over, until they sound like rubber bands stretched across the Grand Canyon. I should be annoyed, but the repetition is a kind of meditation: a reminder that everything begins as noise before it becomes music. I once read that brass players sometimes pack their mouthpieces with wet paper to muffle the sound while they practice, hiding their incompetence the way hermit crabs borrow prettier shells. We are all, in some way, smuggling our ugliness inside something that looks like it belongs.

I keep thinking about the word “threshold.” In architecture it’s the strip of wood or stone you cross to enter a room, but in ecology it’s the moment when an ecosystem flips from one stable state to another—prairie to desert, lake to algae soup. In Old English it was “thersc-wold,” the place where you tread on threshed grain, the powdery edge between food and waste. I wonder how many thresholds I’ve crossed without noticing the threshold-ness of them: the last conversation with my father before the stroke scrambled his nouns, the final night I drank enough to forget the year, the minute I decided not to send the letter that would have ruined two marriages. We expect drama— trumpets, sirens, a spectral hand writing on the wall—but mostly the doors shut behind us with the polite click of a jaw aligning.

Yesterday I found a photograph of myself at seven, wearing a cape made from a bath towel and a crown of dandelions. I am snarling at the camera, bare knees bruised like storm clouds. I don’t remember the day, but I can taste the iron smell of the chain-link fence behind me, feel the way the terry-cloth cape soaked up sun until it was warm as toast against my neck. I want to fold the picture into a paper airplane and sail it back through time, with a note stapled to it: Keep snarling. The things that will try to soften you are not all kind. Also, your future self still owns a cape; it’s just invisible and smells like dish soap and panic attacks.

Sometimes I imagine the air is full of invisible typing, millions of fingertips striking the atmosphere, drafting every possible next sentence of the world. Most of the drafts are discarded—asteroids that never hit, diseases that fizzle, love affairs that remain unspoken across subway cars. But every so often a phrase is committed to the final manuscript: a war, a scientific law, the first time someone calls you by the nickname that will fit better than your given name. I try not to think about the overhead of it, the cosmic cutting room floor strewn with versions of me that married the wrong person, moved to the wrong city, coughed once on the wrong Tuesday and set a different plague spinning.

At night I walk the dog along the creek where the city installed ultraviolet lights to keep the algae from choking. The water glows like a cheap aquarium, radioactive Kool-Aid under a moon that looks embarrassed to be seen with us. The dog pees on every third blade of grass as if negotiating with invisible borders, and I pretend each yellow flag is a tiny treaty, a promise between mammal and earth: I was here, I will not be here long, please remember me as warmth not waste. I talk to her out loud, the dog, tell her things I’d never say to a human because she can’t repeat them and because her eyes already hold the whole dumb universe in them, tail slapping like a metronome set to approximately mercy.

I have a theory that every person is issued one impossible hunger at birth. Not garden-variety yearning—bigger. One kid grows up aching for a color that doesn’t exist, another for the smell of dinosaurs, another for a parent who died the day they were conceived. Mine is a sound: the exact frequency my mother’s bloodstream made when she crossed the kitchen to lift me from the high chair. I chase it in white-noise apps, in the hush between fireworks and their echo, in the static that rises when the coffee maker finishes its cycle. I will never hear it again, but the chasing keeps me vascular, a bat navigating by what isn’t there.

Across town a warehouse of frozen embryos lost power last month when the grid strained under heat no one anticipated. The news ran a single paragraph: 2,000 microscopic possibilities thawed beyond rescue, no longer viable. I can’t stop picturing them as stars winking out in a galaxy the size of a Petri dish, entire civilizations of eyelashes and math skills and bad karaoke choices dissolving into clear jelly. I want to hold a funeral no one would understand, pass out tiny programs printed on rice paper that dissolve on your tongue like communion, taste of nothing, taste of everything that almost.

The trumpet kid just hit a new note, raw and triumphant, like a dented trumpet shouting birthday-candle wish. I raise my broken mug to the window, a toast to the threshold between noise and music, between staying and leaving, between the version of me that finishes this sentence and the one who deletes it halfway through. The coffee is cold. The dog farts in her sleep, a small honk of trust. Somewhere a photon leaves the sun aimed exactly at the crack in my favorite mug, and by the time it arrives I will have decided—again—not to throw it away.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
